lessons.title: You Can't Have It All: Trade-offs
lessons.content:
You already know how design works: you come up with many ideas, then you have to **choose** one. This lesson is about the hard truth behind that choice. Most of the time, you cannot make everything great at once. Improving one thing usually costs you another. That is a **trade-off**: a choice where getting more of one quality means accepting less of another.

Think about buying a phone. You might want it thin, with huge battery life, and cheap. But a bigger battery makes it thicker, and a thinner cheaper phone holds less charge. You can't max out all three. Cars are the same: sporty, fuel-efficient, and roomy pull against each other. A famous shorthand sums it up: **fast, cheap, good — pick two**. You can usually have any two, but not all three together.

Why does this matter? Because the real skill is not wishing for a perfect option that has no downsides. That option rarely exists. The skill is choosing **deliberately**: knowing what you are giving up and deciding it is worth it. A beginner mistake is to assume you can optimize everything at once, or to pick on a gut feeling and only later notice what you sacrificed.

So when you choose, expect to give something up. That is not failure; it is the nature of real choices. Naming the trade-off out loud — 'I am paying more to get it sooner' — turns a blind guess into an informed decision.

items.prompt: What is a 'trade-off'?
choices: A choice where getting more of one quality means accepting less of another
A way to make every quality of a product great at the same time
A drawing of the finished product
A rule that the cheapest option is always best
items.explanation: A trade-off means improving one thing costs you another, like a bigger battery making a phone thicker. It is not a way to maximize everything at once (that is the misconception this whole topic warns against), it is not a drawing, and it does not declare the cheapest option the winner.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'I'll find a phone that is the thinnest, has the longest battery life, and is the cheapest, all at once.' What is the flaw in this thinking?
choices: There is no flaw; you can usually maximize every quality at the same time
The flaw is that thin phones are always the best ones
The flaw is assuming you can optimize everything at once, when these qualities trade off against each other
The flaw is caring about price at all
items.explanation: Thinness, battery life, and price pull against each other, so you cannot max them all out; expecting to is the core beginner mistake. You cannot usually maximize everything at once, thin is not automatically best, and caring about price is perfectly reasonable.

items.prompt: The saying 'fast, cheap, good — pick two' is meant to teach what idea?
choices: That speed is the only thing that ever matters
That you can usually have two of those qualities but not all three at once
That good work is impossible to achieve
That you should always refuse to choose
items.explanation: 'Pick two' captures that these three qualities trade off, so you typically get two and give up the third. It does not say speed is all that matters, that good work is impossible, or that you should never choose; the point is that choosing means giving something up.

items.prompt: Choosing a car that is sporty AND very fuel-efficient AND extremely roomy, all at the top level, is hard mainly because:
choices: Car colors are limited
Sportiness, fuel efficiency, and roominess tend to trade off against one another
Roomy cars are always the cheapest
Fuel efficiency does not matter to anyone
items.explanation: These qualities pull in different directions, so pushing one to the top usually costs another; that is a trade-off. Color choice is unrelated, roomy cars are not automatically cheapest, and fuel efficiency clearly matters to many buyers.

items.prompt: What does it mean to make a choice 'deliberately' when trade-offs are involved?
choices: To pick instantly on a gut feeling and move on
To insist on an option with no downsides at all
To know what you are giving up and decide it is worth it
To let someone else choose so you avoid responsibility
items.explanation: Deliberate choosing means seeing the downside you are accepting and judging it worthwhile. Picking on gut feeling ignores what you sacrifice, insisting on a no-downside option ignores that trade-offs exist, and handing off the choice avoids the decision rather than making it well.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes what usually happens when you choose between real options?
choices: You give something up to gain something else
You always find an option that beats every other on every measure
You never have to compromise if you look hard enough
The first option you see is always the best
items.explanation: Real choices almost always involve giving something up to gain something else; that is the heart of a trade-off. An option that beats all others on every measure rarely exists, looking harder does not erase trade-offs, and the first option is not automatically best.

items.prompt: Saying out loud, 'I am paying more so I can have it sooner,' is a good habit because it:
choices: Proves that price never matters
Hides the downside of the choice
Guarantees you made the cheapest choice
Names the trade-off, turning a blind guess into an informed decision
items.explanation: Naming what you give up (money) for what you gain (speed) makes the decision conscious and informed. It does not prove price never matters, it reveals rather than hides the downside, and it does not guarantee the cheapest choice; in fact it openly chooses to pay more.

items.prompt: A lighter backpack frame is more comfortable to carry but bends more easily, while a heavier frame is sturdier. This is an example of:
choices: A trade-off between weight and sturdiness
A product with no downsides
Proof that heavier is always better
A choice that involves giving up nothing
items.explanation: Lighter versus sturdier is a classic trade-off: improving comfort (less weight) costs strength. It is not a no-downside product, heavier is not always better (it is just a different balance), and the example clearly involves giving something up.

items.prompt: Why is expecting to 'give something up' when you choose NOT a sign of failure?
choices: Because failure only happens when a product is cheap
Because trade-offs are the normal nature of real choices, so a downside is expected, not a mistake
Because good choices actually have zero downsides
Because giving something up means you chose wrong
items.explanation: Since you usually cannot maximize everything, accepting a downside is the normal result of any real choice, not a failure. Cheapness does not define failure, good choices still have downsides, and giving something up is expected rather than proof of a wrong choice.

lessons.title: Making It Explicit: Criteria, Weights, and a Decision Matrix
lessons.content:
Knowing that trade-offs exist is step one. Step two is comparing your options **on purpose** instead of by gut. The tool for this is simple and powerful.

Start by listing the **criteria** — the things that matter for this choice. For a phone that might be price, battery life, and camera quality. Next, decide which criteria matter **most**. Those are your **weights**: a number showing how important each one is. An everyday version is a pros-and-cons list where you put stars next to the things you care about most.

Now build a **decision matrix** (also called a trade study or Pugh chart). It is just a table: your **options are the rows**, your **criteria are the columns**. You **score** each option on each criterion (say 1 to 5, where 5 is best), multiply each score by that criterion's weight, and add up the row. The option with the highest total is a **defensible choice** — one you can explain with numbers, not just a feeling.

Here is one clean example. Suppose price matters most (weight 3) and battery is next (weight 2). Phone B scores 3 on price and 5 on battery: that is 3×3 plus 5×2, which equals 9 plus 10, so 19 points. If a rival phone totals 16, Phone B wins on the criteria you said mattered.

One more rule belongs here. Some requirements are not scored at all — they are **hard constraints**, must-haves that an option either meets or fails. A car that won't fit your garage is out no matter how many points it earns. Check constraints first; score what survives.

items.prompt: In a decision matrix, what are the 'criteria'?
choices: The people who will use the product
The things that matter for the choice, such as price, battery, or quality
The single number that decides the winner
The leftover options you already rejected
items.explanation: Criteria are the qualities that matter for the decision, like price and battery, which become the columns you compare options against. They are not the users, not the final total by itself, and not the rejected options.

items.prompt: What does a 'weight' represent in weighted scoring?
choices: How heavy the physical product is
The price of the cheapest option
How important a criterion is compared to the others
The number of options being compared
items.explanation: A weight is a number showing how much a criterion matters relative to the others, so important things count more in the total. It is not the product's physical weight, not a price, and not a count of options.

items.prompt: What does a decision matrix actually do for you?
choices: It organizes options and weighted scores so you can compare them and pick a defensible winner
It removes the need to use any judgment at all
It guarantees the perfect answer no matter what numbers you put in
It automatically lowers the price of every option
items.explanation: A decision matrix lays out options (rows) and weighted criteria (columns) so you can compare totals and defend your pick with numbers. It supports judgment rather than replacing it, it does not guarantee a perfect answer (bad weights give bad results), and it has nothing to do with changing prices.

items.prompt: In a decision matrix, how are the options and criteria usually arranged?
choices: Options and criteria are both squeezed into a single column
Options are the rows and criteria are the columns
There is no table; you just pick by feel
Criteria are the rows and the winner is hidden
items.explanation: The standard layout puts each option on its own row and each criterion in its own column, with scores filling the grid. Cramming everything into one column, using no table, or hiding the winner all defeat the purpose of organizing the comparison.

items.prompt: Price has weight 3 and battery has weight 2. Phone B scores 3 on price and 5 on battery. What is its weighted total?
choices: 8 (just adding the two scores)
10 (only the battery part)
5 (the highest single score)
items.explanation: You multiply each score by its weight and add: 3×3 is 9, 5×2 is 10, and 9 plus 10 is 19. Adding raw scores (8) ignores the weights, using only battery (10) drops price entirely, and taking the highest single score (5) is not how totals work.

items.prompt: Why is the option with the highest weighted total called a 'defensible' choice?
choices: Because it is always the cheapest one
Because you can explain it with the criteria and scores, not just a gut feeling
Because no one is allowed to question it
Because it was the first option on the list
items.explanation: It is defensible because you can point to the criteria, weights, and scores that justify it, instead of saying 'I just liked it.' It is not necessarily cheapest, it can still be questioned and revised, and its position on the list is irrelevant.

items.prompt: An everyday pros-and-cons list with stars next to the points you care about most is basically a simple version of what?
choices: A criteria-and-weights comparison (a decision matrix)
A random guess with no structure
A way to ignore what matters to you
A rule that the longest list always wins
items.explanation: Stars marking what matters most are weights, and the pros and cons are criteria, so the list is a plain decision matrix. It is not a random guess, it highlights rather than ignores what matters, and the winner is about weighted importance, not list length.

items.prompt: A scored criterion adds points to an option's total. A hard constraint is different because it:
choices: Adds the most points of all
Is a must-have that disqualifies any option failing it, no matter the score
Is just a criterion with a slightly bigger weight
Can be ignored if the total score is high enough
items.explanation: A hard constraint is a pass-or-fail must-have: fail it and the option is out regardless of points, like a car that won't fit the garage. It is not about adding points, it is not merely a heavier-weighted criterion, and a high score does not let you ignore it.

items.prompt: An apartment scores the highest in your matrix, but it does not allow the pet you already own. Pets allowed is a firm must-have. What should you do?
choices: Pick it anyway, since the total score is highest
Raise its score even higher to settle the matter
Treat 'pets allowed' as a hard constraint that disqualifies it, and choose from the options that pass
Delete the pet requirement so the apartment can win
items.explanation: A firm must-have like pets allowed is a hard constraint, so failing it removes the apartment no matter how high it scores; you choose among options that meet the constraint. Picking it anyway ignores the constraint, inflating its score is dishonest, and deleting a real requirement hides a genuine need.

lessons.title: Pitfalls, 'Good Enough,' and Reversible Choices
lessons.content:
A decision matrix helps, but it does not think for you. This lesson covers the traps that fool good people and a few habits that keep your choices sane.

**Watch your biases.** One flashy feature can hijack a whole decision — letting a single number dominate everything else is called **anchoring**. Picking by gut alone, or chasing the **shiny object**, leads you to ignore what actually matters. Keep criteria **measurable**, not vague: 'lasts a full day on one charge' beats 'good battery.' And remember the matrix only reflects the weights you choose — **garbage weights give a garbage answer**. It supports your judgment; it never replaces it.

**Beware diminishing returns.** Pushing one quality to the extreme costs ever more for ever less gain. The last 5% of polish can cost as much as the first 95%. Often **'good enough' beats chasing perfect** — this is called **satisficing**, meaning you pick the first option that clears the bar instead of hunting forever for the ideal. Tie it back to requirements: **meet the requirement, then stop — do not gold-plate.** **Gold-plating** means adding cost and polish nobody asked for.

**Match effort to the stakes.** Some choices are easy to undo (**reversible**); some are hard or costly to undo (**irreversible**). When you are unsure, prefer cheap, reversible choices — you can change your mind. Renting before buying lets you test a neighborhood without being locked in. Save your deep analysis for the costly, hard-to-reverse decisions.

In short: name your criteria, weight what matters, compare honestly, respect hard constraints, avoid bias and gold-plating, and spend your effort where the choice is costly and hard to undo.

items.prompt: Letting one flashy feature dominate your whole decision is a bias called:
choices: Anchoring
Weighting
Prototyping
Iterating
items.explanation: Anchoring is when a single feature or number grabs too much attention and skews the whole choice. Weighting is the deliberate, balanced step of assigning importance; prototyping and iterating come from the design loop and are not biases.

items.prompt: Why is 'good battery' a weaker criterion than 'lasts a full day on one charge'?
choices: Because shorter phrases are always better
Because 'good battery' is vague, while a full day on one charge is measurable
Because battery life does not matter
Because vague criteria score higher automatically
items.explanation: Measurable criteria like 'a full day on one charge' can be checked and compared fairly, while 'good battery' is vague and means different things to different people. Phrase length is irrelevant, battery life clearly matters, and vague criteria do not earn higher scores; they just cause confusion.

items.prompt: What does the warning 'garbage weights give a garbage answer' mean for using a decision matrix?
choices: The matrix is useless and should never be used
The matrix is always right no matter what you enter
If your weights and scores are careless, the result is too; the matrix supports judgment but does not replace it
You should always give every criterion the same weight
items.explanation: The matrix only reflects the weights and scores you feed it, so poor inputs produce a poor answer; it organizes judgment rather than replacing it. It is not useless, it is not automatically right, and forcing equal weights would ignore that some criteria matter more.

items.prompt: What is 'diminishing returns'?
choices: The point where a product becomes free
Pushing a quality further costs more and more while adding less and less benefit
A rule that more effort always gives proportionally more quality
The idea that the first bit of effort is wasted
items.explanation: Diminishing returns means each extra push toward perfection costs more for smaller and smaller gains, like the last 5% of polish costing as much as the first 95%. It is not about things becoming free, effort does not always pay off proportionally, and early effort usually gives the biggest gains, not wasted ones.

items.prompt: Why can 'good enough' (satisficing) beat chasing the perfect option?
choices: Because perfect options are illegal
Because the extra cost of chasing perfect often buys very little real gain once you meet the requirement
Because quality never matters
Because the most expensive choice is always best
items.explanation: Once an option meets the requirement, pushing further hits diminishing returns, so the added cost buys little; picking the first option that clears the bar saves time and money. Perfect options are not illegal, quality does matter (the bar must still be met), and the priciest choice is not automatically best.

items.prompt: Adding extra cost and polish that nobody asked for, beyond what the requirement needs, is called:
choices: Satisficing
Meeting the requirement
Gold-plating
A hard constraint
items.explanation: Gold-plating is piling on cost and polish beyond the requirement, which wastes effort for little gain. Satisficing is the opposite (stopping once it is good enough), meeting the requirement is the goal itself, and a hard constraint is a must-have, not extra polish.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a reversible and an irreversible decision?
choices: A reversible decision is easy or cheap to undo; an irreversible one is hard or costly to undo
A reversible decision is always the wrong one
An irreversible decision costs nothing to change
There is no real difference between them
items.explanation: Reversible decisions can be undone cheaply, while irreversible ones are hard or expensive to take back, which is why they deserve more analysis. A reversible choice is not automatically wrong, irreversible choices are costly to change, and the difference is very real.

items.prompt: When you are unsure, why does renting a place before buying fit the 'reversible choice' idea?
choices: Because renting is always cheaper over a lifetime
Because renting lets you test the choice and change your mind, instead of locking in a costly hard-to-undo decision
Because buying can always be undone for free
Because reversibility means you should never commit to anything
items.explanation: Renting is a cheap, reversible way to test a neighborhood while uncertain, leaving room to change your mind, whereas buying is costly and hard to reverse. Renting is not always cheaper long-term, buying is not free to undo, and reversibility is about matching effort to stakes, not avoiding all commitment.

items.prompt: Which choice is the clearest example of a decision-making pitfall?
choices: Listing measurable criteria before comparing options
Buying the phone purely because one flashy feature caught your eye, ignoring price and battery
Checking hard constraints before scoring
Stopping once an option meets the requirement
items.explanation: Letting one shiny feature drive the whole purchase while ignoring what matters is the anchoring and shiny-object pitfall. Listing measurable criteria, checking constraints first, and stopping at good enough are all sound practices, not pitfalls.
